submission.title: Nepali Parenting is really really bad
submission.body:
I am 12th grader science students. I have mostly spending most of my life in hostel and now when I came out. I find the life here is more shitty than it was in hostel. I am ambitious and I wanted to do things that matters not shitty college programs that are doing nothing than promoting the college and are full of mindless and soulness body where all people are marching either with peer pressure.

And I finally want to share my problems, my concerns, my joys and my interest through my blog. I am really grateful apart from all the criticism i have been getting like aaile yo garne haina, formal education re , degree ani all .

Anyway happy that my life's not shitty enough to suicide.

Can you guys share me what you feel about nepali parenting styles (i am writing a article on 'Nepal: 5 Bad Parenting Practices')

comments.body:
Thanks for making a submission. Please use an appropriate flair for better reach and response. In case of NSFW post, use "sax sux" flair and tag it as NSFW. Otherwise, the post will be removed.

*I am a bot, and this action was performed automatically. Please [contact the moderators of this subreddit](/message/compose/?to=/r/NepalSocial) if you have any questions or concerns.*
i think asians or anyone nearing this *style* shouldn’t have kids

Parenting is generally difficult and I find that once you stop blaming your parents for everything wrong in this world, learn to accept that they are (possibly flawed) humans, you liberate yourself from this chain of thought. Having said that, all bad parenting practice can be boiled down to this one thing: not thinking from the perspective of a child.

but even the perspective of parents is wrong. I think there is always from the corner of their heart they all feel that what they are doing is wrong but they are never taught to stand up for themselves or better no one in Nepal is provided with the courage to stand for themselves..

Sometimes people should take a step back and think, is it the Nepali parents or is it just my parents?

People be having shitty parents and still be in denial that it's their parents that are bad not everyone else is. 

Though all Nepali parents share some common traits but that doesnt mean a particular parent or parenting style reflects every Nepali parents.
There is a really famous book on parenting: *How to talk so kids will listen and listen so kids will talk*. You may find some information if you look there.
yeah i agree but it's on view if u see particularly everyone is different every style , every idea , every parents but if you see as one you can see every human being as a single being.
thanks a lot.

> Nepali Parenting is really really bad

What is parenting that is really really good?

parenting that provides children ample space to explore their world without imposing strict rules or limitations or parenting that allows children to find their own paths without pushing them into predetermined molds. or parenting that actually loves

Well, there's truth to having a degree equals more jobs. More high paying oppurtunities. When you're sending your resume to companies that know what they're looking for, a degree in marketing or mass communication will go a long way than a self-taught marketer, provided both have similar kinds of experience. 

In professional circles, a bachelor's degree in a relevant field is a prerequisite for most jobs. In Nepal, you'll find folks without a bachelors degree doing all things in IT, but trust me, 10 or 20 years from now when the education will hopefully catch up with industry, the market will be saturated with qualified folks who already have a degree. You will be at a disadvantage.

Formal education is only bad if you get into Economics when you'd be much happier designing circuits.

Have you been earning through your blog? Is that sufficient to support you? From what I gather, you depend on your parents for finances. Could you move out tomorrow and pay your expenses with your blog?

Jati tight bau aama tyeti nei fataa chhora chhori vanchhan, yo saga chai ma ekdam agree garchu(in most of the cases). Parents shouldn't control everything about their children. They should let their children explore for themselves. They should be a guide in the path their children choose to move. 

J kura ma ni kutne chai ekdam naramro kura ho. Many parents think of scolding and beating is the only solution to prevent their children from doing something they don't want them to do. Well, it works only for some time, but it has a limit.

idk if it is Nepali parenting or just my parents but humiliating and threatening to humiliate publicly had been a big thing growing up.   
  
other thing is body shaming. was a fat kid, gained confidence after losing a lot of weight during teen. again gained weight during boards cuz of stress and all. nightmares came back. yk who was fueling it all. (thanks mom)  
  
comparing with others and criticizing constantly. "Criticism, like rain, should be gentle enough to nourish a man’s growth without destroying his roots."

expecting kids to fulfill their dreams. met a lot of ppl forced to take a stream/course just to satisfy their parent's dream.

"what will others think" yk it.

Most Nepali parents don't even know how one can earn money(from different domains) and live a happy life; they just know something secure and have thikthak amount of money and just want their child to be secure and safe and not get involved in other risky areas. 

So if they know something good for you and still oppose you to move towards that, I will consider that bad parenting. They don't know in the majority of the case so, you know what I mean.

You have to convince them or prove them why do you want to do what you want to do. Like If you want to do digital marketing, and suppose your parents are over 60 years old so they may not know what that mean and they probably don't know any other person doing the same so not sure about you doing good in that field. 

And of course, there are some parents who are strict without any point.

Nepali parenting is low-consciousness tribalistic parenting.

Parenting by force, threatening words, hate, anger, egoistic, which causes depression, anxiety, and emotional dysfunction in kids.

Nepali parents are not sensitive, gentle, and not kind to their kids. Their main concern is that their kids will get ruined because of the culture or other kids and threaten them in various ways which ultimately leads to their emotional breakdown which leads to ruin.

Nepali parents do not provide a protective safe space to discuss things. They are emotionally underdeveloped and emotionally immature, and can't handle a child's emotional and psychological needs.

This leaves children traumatized and in constant anxiety over life's challenges. 

Hitting should be a big no-no. 

Parents SHOULD NOT hit their children. That's the first step.

They hit because they were hit by their parents, and low-consciousness people have an innate need to repeat patterns (as they terrified of new and unknown such as Love).

Cycle continues.

Break the inter-generational trauma.

Take a pledge to not hit.
some people are not meant to be parents yet they become one. especially those who project their dreams through their children.

Well, we don’t have a concept of freedom and personal choices and privacy and stuffs. They wanna have  control over your life, even getting you to marry someone of their choice. Good thing is they help you financially and will leave you inheritance if they have any. Personally, I don’t wanna live with my parents after I graduate college and get a job, they are nice people to live not controlling and probably retire without much financial problem. I guess western parenting style is much better.
Parents might know how to treat their child  don't they

hey just read your response. thank for your response. It seemed me like if you are thinking on other aspects than I asked but yeah well future is unpreditable and yeah if education become flawless, equal and good for everyone of course market will the saturated with the ones with degree but creating a society of harmony and welcoming everyone is the main thing that education should teach and if it is taught maybe it won't be a disadvantage and education is not about facts. It it about path showing and it is about self-exploration. As a human being we are never superior enough to teach others all we can do is to provide the young's ones with the true upbringing.

And no I don't earn from the blog but yeah it is eligible and advertisements have started so my earning is being collected in the moment.

submission.title: Looking for opinions about baha'i religion
submission.body: Hello Everyone. I recently came across baha'i religion today. I am planning to write an article about it and it's beliefs along with it's presence in nepali community. Have you heard about this religion, if you have heard what are your stances about this. Be as open and please share criticisms if you have it too.

comments.body: It is false religion
False as in what?

I think the tenets of the faith are admirable. Some of the laws are not. Overall, it's a good thing. 

A more specific question may yield more specific answers.
How many true religions do you recognize? Are all religions false except the religion you follow?

Yeah , all other religions are false and i can explain… or explain by showing authentic scholarly explanations to make it faster and simple….

I can prove it to you that bible was later on corrupted… etc etc…. in short islam is right …

It can make ur journey much more faster if u open this link if u are truthful in finding a change of life in all aspects and finding the truth…. 

https://quran.com       ——> for quran in arabic + translation 

https://quran411.com/surah-maidah

^
For roman english and + translation and arabic 


DO NOT ARGUE WITHOUT OPENING THOSE LINKS AND READING IT AT LEAST 2-3%
FALSE IN ALL ASPECTS , SHOW COMPELLING EVIDENCE TO ARGUE TO PROVE UR RELIGION IS CORRECT….. BUT EVENTUALLY IT WONT BE CORRECT….

I'm obviously biased here, but I love it! I do think the community still has a lot of growing to do, but we're a young faith and I'm proud to see thousands of other Bahá'ís doing their part to both enrichen our own tradition and also be as efficient as possible in serving humanity
Can you tell it's stances about sexuality?
are there any practices like poojas in hindu in baha'i or any festivals?
Huh? Weren't you talking about Baha'i

Sorry I'm not sure I fully understand. I believe you're asking if practices like puja that are found in the Bahá'í faith. The answer for that would be no. While the faith confirms the truth of Hinduism and the validity of the practice of puja in the past as a way for people to worship God directly through the means of the murti (the image of God), the Bahá'í faith states that at this point in the evolution of humanity such practices aren't needed and on the contrary can lead to simple idol worship rather than proper puja being done. So these practices are typically advised against in the Bahá'í faith. However we don't view Hindu Temples having puja as sinful in any way, but rather people simply practicing something that's a bit out of date. Just in the same way that we see no need for doing 5 prayers a day like in Islam anymore, however there's nothing technically wrong if someone chooses to do so (though if they were a Bahá'í then some specific restrictions might be double checked to make sure the person isn't somehow thinking this makes them a better follower of the faith).

Hopefully that answers your question. If I misunderstood then I apologize and please correct me. Otherwise feel free to ask any more questions you may have. I love these types of discussions
Thanks for your response. Actually I am looking for if there is any specific customs or festivals believers of baha'i celebrate?

Oh of course. We have Naw Rúz which is basically our New Years (since we run off the Badí calendar). 

We then have Rídvan, which is a 12 day long celebration marking the time that Bahá'u'lláh announced Himself as the awaited Manifestation of God.

We have the 19-day Fast, which occurs around Spring and basically is the new period of fasting commandes of us rather than keeping the Islamic tradition of Ramadam (since the Bahá'í faith stemmed from Islam). We still respect the idea of Ramadan, but we acknowledge that every new Messenger/Manifestation of God may present new laws for the followers to adhere to. The Jewish faith has its fasting days, yet at the advent of Islam then Ramadan was a new fast commanded by God. So the same with the 19-day Fast, which is a bit shorter and more lenient than Ramadan since it's believed there's no more need of such long and strict fasting.

Like most faiths we celebrate the birth and death of our founders, which would be the Báb and Bahá'u'lláh. We also celebrate the day of the Declaration of the Báb, which is when the Báb announced Himself as the Manifestation of God (in Islam called al-Mahdi). 

And as well we celebrate the days that 'Abdu'l-Bahá, son of Bahá'u'lláh and leader of the faith after Bahá'u'lláh's passing, himself was appointed the succesor of Bahá'u'lláh and we also celebrate the date of his passing.

Also every 19 days we come together for a Feast, this is our version of Church Sunday or Friday prayers. Just as previous revelations have provided new calendar systems for their followers to use, such as the Islamic calendar which differs from the one used by Jews and Christians. So similarly we were given the Badí calendar, which has 19 months in total and each month has 19 days, hence why we have the Feast every 19 days (though we often also meet more often for events like study circles and such). Each of these months is named after an attribute of God as well. Which this system, it creates a year of 361 days, so four less days than a solar year. So we finally also have the four-day long celebration of Ayyám-i-Há, which makes up for those four missing days, often this celebration involves gift-giving

For a more in-depth explaination of the Badí calendar system and the holidays I gave above, this site is a good and simple resource: https://www.bahai.us/events-calendar/holy-days/

I'm no expert on the Baha'i faith, but I do know that the laws prohibit sex outside of marriage.  Also, they don't recognize gay marriage.  Unmarried gay sex is the same as unmarried straight sex (in terms of the law), but married gay sex is impossible because the faith does not permit two people of the same sex to marry.

I'm not a theist, so theistic let alone monotheistic religions aren't in my wheelhouse. But I have interacted with several Baha'i on various forums. 


They are an interesting bunch. Generally friendly but with stances I don't share on sexual purity and women in leadership positions (no women can sit the highest position of Universal House of Justice, though theu can in lower offices.)


Baha'i believe that all religions speak about the same divine source, and are in that way not exclusivist. Just because you're not a Baha'i doesn't mean you're bad or hell bound. Just on a different stage of your journey. 


However they also have a terrible habit of trying to convert/"unify" other religious people by saying Hinduism/Islam/Judaism/Buddhism/etc is just another pathway to Baha'i. This has the effect of flattening their knowledge of other religions into whatever they can compare to their own. I had to explain to one that darkness =/= evil and lightness =/= good in Taoism, the taiji or yin yang requires both in balance, so you need and respect both light and dark. There are also lots of religions that don't have gods, or the gods they do have are nothing like a monotheistic or even henotheistic one. They don't really listen to that part, intent to focus on what is similar to their path, even if they have to make it up. Lol. 


Anyway, aside from that, their list of virtues is a lot more tolerable than the 10 commandments, not having exclusivity built in and generally being in the vein of 'don't be a jerk and leave things better than you found it.'

One of their stated aims is the "Most Greatest Peace" where the entire world or the majority thereof will be Baha'i and follow the Baha'i laws and have a Baha'i government.

Their founder Mírzá Ḥusayn-\`Alí Núrí reportedly claimed to be the second coming of Jesus, Mohammed and Krishna

They talk a good game but ask them how they reconcile the resurrection of christ (hint - according to the Baha'i view - it is "metaphorical") with the Islamic view on the subject.

I am biased but to me it is a hastily put together attempt at syncretism of Islam and Christianity with grandiose ideas about itself;  but in reality which has some old fashioned ideas in its main book Kitab-i-Aqdas
Bahai religion is nonsense

submission.title: Looking for opinions about baha'i religion
submission.body: Hello Everyone. I recently came across baha'i religion today. I am planning to write an article about it and it's beliefs along with it's presence in nepali community. Have you heard about this religion, if you have heard what are your stances about this. Be as open and please share criticisms if you have it too.

comments.body:
https://bahai.org.np/


Should be a good place to start for the nepali community.

There is a strong and growing but still small Baha'i community in Nepal, particularly in one region.  In 2023, the Universal House of Justice announced that a local House of Worship would be planned and built in Kanchanpur, Nepal.  There has been a lot of  growth in the past 20 years.  Nepal historically was quite restrictive and did not allow active efforts to convert others to a religion, including teaching the Baha'i Faith.  The following is dated and does not reflect the growth in the past 20 years:  [https://bahaipedia.org/Nepal](https://bahaipedia.org/Nepal) See also [https://bicentenary.bahai.org/the-bab/country-celebrations/nepal/](https://bicentenary.bahai.org/the-bab/country-celebrations/nepal/) for more recently celebrations and images from communities in Nepal as well as the official site provided by u/papadjeef .  There are Baha'is among the Nepalese community in parts of the US.  \[My daughter in Texas has close friends who are Nepalese and Baha'is.\]

[Bahai.org](http://Bahai.org) is the official site and has a lot of information.

For me, the most commonly referred to introduction is Baha'u'llah and the New Ear.  You can download or read it online at:  [https://www.bahai.org/library/other-literature/publications-individual-authors/bahaullah-new-era/](https://www.bahai.org/library/other-literature/publications-individual-authors/bahaullah-new-era/)

There is a shorter introduction by Dr. Momen at:  [https://bahai-library.com/momen\_short\_introduction\_bahai](https://bahai-library.com/momen_short_introduction_bahai)

There is a site that has a collection of links to papers and summaries of the Baha'i Faith, introductory texts, and some videos about the Baha'i Faith.  [https://bahai-library.com/introductory\_information](https://bahai-library.com/introductory_information)   It can be a bit overwhelming at first and a lot to absorb.  Our religion is modern, much more documented, and authenticated and has a far larger and more extensive Scripture called the Baha'i Writings.  [https://bahai-library.com/bic\_the\_bahais\_magazine](https://bahai-library.com/bic_the_bahais_magazine) is a magazine style intro to the Faith linked to on that site.  [https://religionlink.com/source-guides/the-bahai-faith-a-source-guide-for-reporters/](https://religionlink.com/source-guides/the-bahai-faith-a-source-guide-for-reporters/) is a short summary for reporters.

[https://www.bahai.org/video/light-to-the-world](https://www.bahai.org/video/light-to-the-world) is an hour long video about Baha'u'llalh and how His teachings affect the current community

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GPKEkSXhtgw&t=20s](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GPKEkSXhtgw&t=20s)  Really good movie about the Bab with discussions by a number of Baha'i academics.

[https://news.bahai.org/story/1551/](https://news.bahai.org/story/1551/)   Hour long about 'Abdu'l-Baha
Read the book The Baha'i Faith the emerging global religion by William s Hatcher and j. Douglas Martin
It looks like you have got it covered. Good work!
